speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 5, interview with Vera Ray. You were talking about coming to Australia and standing out in your clothes, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, god, yes, and eating the wrong food. And I, I remember feeling really bad. And I don't know what sort of bad-- depressed. Depressed, angry, furious, and isolated. And my memory was that I didn't want to talk to anyone. And I remember eating my lunch alone under the stairs in the library.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I happened to meet, about a year ago, somebody, and we recognized each other. And she said to me, you know that I have been feeling guilty about you for the last 40 years? I said, no, why? What have you done? She said, well, I was one of the girls that ignored you and that laughed at you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to her, but I thought that I didn't want to know you people. And she said, no, no, we thought you were just the strangest thing on earth, and we really made fun of you. So there you go, a little bit of evidence here, you know. If we wouldn't have been so paranoid about being Jewish, I think I would have had an easier transition period. Because there were some nice Jewish girls who wanted to take me under their wing. And one minute I told them I was Jewish, and the next minute I told them that they didn't hear me, that I wasn't Jewish. That was really-- yeah, it was a bad time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I met my future husband, you know, when I was only 16. I wasn't quite, I wasn't quite 17. And I got married too early and everything like that. And I think it's because I was just lonely, and you know, I didn't really have a proper teenage-hood. So yeah, I went to university. And halfway through my course, I got married. And before I collected my, my, my degree, I was breastfeeding my daughter. So it was all a little bit too quick. And, you know, I think that if I would have stayed in Czechoslovakia, it would have been slower. Did you experience directly anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Australia? Oh, yes. Oh, yes. You know, apart from a drunk screaming at me, dirty Jew, in a, in a tram, which was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, I didn't expect it in Australia. I did expect it in Australia. I expected it anywhere, but I was told that it will be OK. And then at school, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, my English was nonexistent just about. I knew a maximum of 20 words when we, when we got here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there I was learning Shakespeare. And the second term-- we did the first term and the next term. And my mother said, you'll have to have tuition. So I went to the city to a college to learn

speaker: interviewee
text: Now is all about Shylock, OK. And, boy, did the, did the teacher give me a hard time. He made me read the nasty bits over and over and over again. And I said to my mother, I'm not going back. So that was another experience that I had. They were the--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think that they knew that you are Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but they did. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and you know, I don't, I don't know whether it was because I was Jewish or because I was a foreigner, hard to know. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But those two things were specific, you know. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] And again, we said we weren't Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was very confusing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it for your parents to settle in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the times of assimilation. There was not the concept of multiculturalism. And my mother was the best assimilator you would ever meet. She wanted to, you know, be part of the woodwork, because that is the way-- that was her way of survival.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I mean, I think that's what killed her, you know. Anti-Semitism and the war killed my mother 50 years, 40 years later. Because she, she could never get rid of her anxiety. She could never get-- rid herself of, of fear of, if they found out I'm Jewish, what will happen? She wasn't a person who lived in the past in any other respect, but this fear of, of, of, of being discovered that she's Jewish, that was-- I couldn't believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: She, she stayed in a-- the last couple of years of her life, she was in a hostel, a beautiful place. You know, we always thought that if every person in Australia could live the way she, she did, it would be good. I'd be very happy. I mean, they had everything laid on, and they had food, and if they wanted to, to cook, they could cook. And if they didn't want-- you know, everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she wouldn't mix with the other people. She would not mix. She isolated herself. Why? In case they found out she was Jewish. So she was still living in the war. That makes me so angry, you know, what the war had, had done to this woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yes, I think it's anxiety that killed her. And she always said to me, don't, don't tell anyone. Because I married my, my husband, who was also Jewish. And that was-- she wanted me to marry out. Out, this will never happen again. And you know, didn't live up to her expectations. So she-- on the surface, she settled in very well. She got a job. She got friends. But she was in hiding. How can you settle well in hiding? You're only safe because they don't know who you are. And my father, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father was held back by my mother. Don't go there. Don't do this. They will find out, you know? So I have to be honest. He would have had a different life if she wouldn't have been so frightened. Yep. And I caught this fear for years, for years until the '70s, the women's liberation movement, the Vietnam War. I suddenly woke up like from a bad dream. Hey, you know, they're not going to kill me, and if they are, so what? I don't want to hide anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I started to identify as a lesbian, and again I was in the closet. Again, I was frightened. And then finally I thought, I don't want to do this anymore. And I've turned my fear into courage. And it doesn't mean that I'm not scared. It just means that my courage is greater than my fear. So sometimes it's very stressful.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying that you had a child when you were studying.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Vivien, who I hope will be here soon. Yes, and then my son was born two years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tony. Well, it's Antony, but it's Tony.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever manage to finish your study?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. You know, once I make up my mind about something, I just keep going. I finished-- so I got my degree and then when the children-- I read Spock. And Spock said, you have to be home with the children until they go to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I obediently stayed home for seven years, and suffered what they call housewives' blues, which is now translated as depression, probably clinical depression at that. And I got out of it when somebody said to me, hey, you're too bright to stay home. Go do something. So I managed to do a part-time course, a diploma of education. And then when the kids were old enough, they went to school. When the kids were old enough, they went to school. And when they were old enough, I went to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I started to teach. And I taught high school for 22 years until I became allergic to teenagers and said, not another teenager. And I went back to school, back to university, and retrained as a psychologist. And I've been working with-- in, in the counseling field for the last six years. So I went back to school when I was quite old-ish. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Again, a little bit of my courage and a little bit of my fear propelled me, because I didn't want to waste my time and my life anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you live now?

speaker: interviewee
text: How do I live now? Very well, thank you. Well, at the moment, I live in this house. And I have been able to create a very wonderful and supportive community around me. I, when I, when I moved in here, I built a studio out the back, and a very dear friend of mine has moved in. And I managed to be quite instrumental in my friend buying the house, my friend, Carmen, buying the house over the fence. And we've put a little gate through. And my dearest friend-- they're all my dear friends. I don't know if you can hierarchically structure friends. But the friend that I've had for longer, for 20 years, has bought a house two doors down from the back. And her child is called Pia, and, and she is my honorary grandchild, or I'm her honorary granddaughter. She asked me how is it that I'm her grandmother, and I said to her, well, we adopted each other when you were a baby. She's quite happy with that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I have one biological granddaughter and one non-biological granddaughter. And I have just created a community here. And every time I hear of a friend who wants to move, I say, you must move, move into our little community, into Alphington.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, I don't know, they seem to be doing it. So we now have four houses, four, four households. And my daughter just lives around the corner, and my son lives a little bit far away. But it's not that far. It's in St. Kilda. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'm good friends with my ex-husband and his partner, and I have a partner, and we all live happily ever after.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talk a lot already about it, but I just wanted to ask particularly, how do you think that your war experience affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's a big one. It made me very frightened, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very frightened of many things for a very long time. And, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] and I didn't know how to talk about my-- I didn't know where to slot my experiences in. And my-- when I-- I sort of-- you know, the '70s were very important, because I discovered a framework that I can slot things in. And I started to think politically about how the world is structured. And I have a feminist analysis of how the world is structured. And so it makes sense to me in, in, in that, in that terminology. And this has given me strength to basically grow in courage. And I do believe that fear and courage are very wound up in me, because I have very great fears, and I-- it's by my decision that I will overcome them. Because my mother couldn't overcome them, you know. My father had more courage than fear. My mother had more fear than courage. And I made the choice that I will cultivate my courage so it is always a little bit bigger than my fears.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's made my life complicated, basically. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And it did not give me courage, you know. I did-- I got no courage from the war. I just got fear.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the courage I got from a very supportive feminist framework. In the olden days, we used to call it sisterhood. Now, of course, it's, it's not-- it's no longer-- in the, in the postmodernist eras, we don't talk about sisterhood. But I still-- I have created a sisterhood here, you know, and I let in a few brothers as well, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that is an answer to how did, how did the war affect me. It ruined a lot of my years of my life when I was in hiding, just like my mother. And I didn't know what to do with it. It is just-- I think I am overly-- I take overly-- I take more risks now than I need to to compensate. I have very few secrets, because I think secrets are such a burden.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I feel very free now because-- ask me something, I'll tell you. Very, very few secrets, if any, probably if any. I have some private things that I don't divulge. But if somebody said to me, look, it's important for me to know about this, I'd say, OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So the war has given me fear, and the peace has given me courage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I had to create that peace, you know. It didn't come from anywhere. It came from finding out finally, you know, for those years I wanted to know, what is life about? Why am I here? Why did I have to go through the war? Why am I alive? Why aren't so many people not alive? You know? And being, being married to a survivor, my goodness, me, you know? We had 20 years of asking questions-- 22 years-- or of avoiding questions. But it's the same thing. Did you tell your children about the experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'd made a early decision, and my husband agreed with me, that they will have the childhood that we missed out on. And now, they have to find out from scratch. And my son is not interested. And that's all right. That's his choice. But at least he had as good a childhood as I could give him. Because other members of my family did it differently, and I think their children had a very disturbed childhood. So I'm happy that they were-- that they knew nothing. Not totally happy, mind you, because they have lost-- they have lost the privilege of being members of the Jewish community. And if they want it, they'll have to work hard to get it. But they have a right to be there if they want to.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's now more than 50 years since the end of the war. What message would you give to your children and to your grandchildren and to the world that would listen about the war, about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stand up and fight. And if you lose your life, you haven't lost your dignity. Don't give in to fear. Fear is much more dangerous than death. And if they can just learn to be who they are, and to be who they are with pride, then my survival was worthwhile. Otherwise, it doesn't matter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So go out there and stand up for what you believe in and stand up for who you are. For what you believe in, maybe you'll change your mind, but who you are, you are. If you're Jewish, you're Jewish. If you're a lesbian, you're a lesbian.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And don't let anyone put you down [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for that. If you're a bad person, OK, feel guilty. Don't feel guilty for something that is not wrong, that is you. I don't believe in God, you know, but there was a picture once I saw, a little boy, and he said, God don't make no junk. And that's my message.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for giving your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'd like to introduce half of my family. The other half isn't here. This is Vivien Ray, my daughter. And this is Danika Ray, my granddaughter. And she's also going to introduce a part of our family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Tuppy.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. That's Tuppy. And I also have a non-biological granddaughter, Pia, who can't be here, and my son, Tony. But otherwise, we're all here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vivien, did your mother tell you about her experiences through the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she didn't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She brought my brother and myself up, not as Jewish people, but more-- well, she gave us a bit of Christianity just to find out a bit about what Christians believe and to have something. We have a-- we both have a baptism certificate and a confirmation certificate.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't know that she was Jewish or that my brother and I were Jewish until I was probably a late teenager. Before that, I knew that my father and all my cousins and aunties and uncles were Jewish. So I learned a little bit about it through my extended family, but I didn't know very much. And since then, did you hear about the experiences that your mother went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Since then I have. We've spoken a lot, and I've seen a, a videotape that's been made from a different source. And yes, since, since I've been around 20 years old, we've been talking a lot about it. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What impact did it have on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the time, when I first found out that my mother was Jewish, it, it didn't have a great impact on me for the first few years. I was just thinking about it. But I guess over the last 10 years or so, I've been wanting to find out more and more. I've been asking a lot of questions. I've been trying to decide for myself whether I'm Jewish or not. I know that the religion says that I am. And so the impact now is that I feel rather confused. I feel like I have a choice. I don't have to be Jewish if I don't want to, but I, I do feel like I am. And, and now I'm curious and inquisitive, and I do want to know much more about the Jewish culture, not about the religion, but about the culture. Yeah. And we've spoken about how, really, if Hitler hadn't existed, then I would not be in this confused state. [LAUGHS] I would have been brought up Jewish, and there would have been no need to protect my brother and I. And, and I would have learned about the rituals and, and been, been brought up with-- in a Jewish family. But I realize the-- that there is the fear and the protective side of why that didn't happen. And I really value that decision that my parents made, that my mother made that-- you know, not for us not to be brought up as Jews. And I really understand that and, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and value that, that decision.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a relief.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Yeah. But-- so now I'm going through [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] inquiries of my own, yeah. And Danika, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] again, if I'm Jewish, then she's Jewish. But we don't know very much about it, so it kind of feels odd to say that we're Jewish and not know much about it. [LAUGHS] But I guess in a few years we'll know a lot more.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think that you knowing about what your mother went through during the war as a child affected your relationship with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think I see her as a very positive person, always looking on the bright side of things. When there's a choice about seeing something as negative, as the glass half empty, she would choose to see the glass as half full. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So, I would very much like to be like her. I think in a lot of ways I am. And that's something that I really love about her. And I think that that does relate to her war experiences, plus probably other things as well, but escaping and hiding and getting through it all, survival I'm sure contributes to her enormous sense of optimism and enthusiasm for life, which I really believe has been passed on to me, and hope that it has.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'd like to keep passing it down the generations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think that knowing about the war and about the Holocaust is influencing the way you are bringing your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: It does influence me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, again, it influences me in terms of Jewish identity. But she-- I mean, she chooses for herself. And at the age of almost seven, she's choosing to identify as Jewish. When she knows more and understands it more, maybe at a, an older age, she would choose something else. But it's really up to her. I just give her information. But it does affect me, because, you know, I don't want to give her heaps of information. I know that other people, other grandparents and other parents, do tell their children everything. And I guess the fact that I wasn't taught everything-- I'm telling her more than that, but I still hold back. I'm still being protective. I'm passing on this notion of protection. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Age-appropriate information, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's probably what it is, because I do it in other things, the news and--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --other things that are happening in the world that I want to protect her from. And this is just one of the things. But I know it's different from other people, other Jewish families. Some.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some. I just thought I'd better put that in-- some other Jewish families. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you would like to add, Vera?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's pretty good, isn't she? I think we're a bit of a mutual admiration society. I, I admire Vivien very much for, for the, for the things that I value. And that is courage. She's very brave. And she has fears, and she overcomes them, too, which is what I spoke about before, Viv. And, and I admire that, because I think it is an incredibly important survival mechanism, because I think that life in itself is only important if it is lived like a mensch. And people who will watch this video will know what that means to live like a mensch.

speaker: interviewee
text: And yes, I'd, I'd really just like to say that I do think my mother is wonderful. I think she's a wonderful mother, and I think she's a wonderful grandmother and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Gee, what, what do I say now, Dan?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] And I, I do feel very lucky to have been born to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gee, thank you. I feel very lucky that you have turned out the way you've turned out, and I feel very lucky that I've got a beautiful granddaughter called Danika Ray.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will there be a, a message that you would give to your daughter and, and to the people that would listen about the war and about Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the message is really an anti-racism message [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and an anti-homophobia message and an anti- any minority group message. Yeah, but really, if we could all learn to live together with our own differences, realize that we, we have different beliefs and different opinions and different lifestyles, and that's OK. Yeah. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, I, of course, I hope that there will be, never be another World War, never be another madman with so much power. And I do hope that when such things start arising, even just the slightest bit like they have started in Australia, that people have been given the freedom to express their phobias and their negative sides about certain people because of skin color or race, I hope that people stand up very quickly against that as, again, they have done in rallies and demonstrations and letters to the editor and that kind of thing. But I, I really would encourage people to take a stand very quickly to stop it from even moving an inch.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll drink to that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
